topic.title: Webservice Calls with doc() and unparsed-text()

body.p:
A common practice is to use the function document() or short doc() to include remote resources in the transformation. In a Schematron validation, for example, a rule like:
UWE test footnote (30/40, callout D): DITA fn maps to UWE footnote/key/desc.
body.pre: <sch:not-assert id="personal-check" role="error" test="doc(concat('https://tekturcms.de/personal.xqy?personal-id=',personal-id))/kuendigung"> Angestellter mit ID "<sch:value-of select="personal-id"/>" hat gekündigt! </sch:not-assert>
body.p: would call a remote web service and check whether a termination has been filed for the employee with personal-id. If this is the case, the negative assertion not-assert is not fulfilled, and the Schematron rule fires - which in the simplest case should probably result in a log file entry.

What presumably many do not yet know - I am simply assuming here that my current state of knowledge corresponds to that of the majority of XML developers - is the fact that the function unparsed-text() also takes a URL as a parameter:
While with doc() or document() a returned XML fragment is processed, unparsed-text() expects, for example, a JSON string, which can then be converted to XML using the json-to-xml() function.
For example, the counterpart to the angestellter template could be implemented in XQuery as follows:
(More on XQuery and the constructs used here, such as render-response())
The returned JSON document then looks like this:
Converted to XML, you get a map structure:
which explains the selector expression in the above XPath:
The result of the conversion would be - as expected - an <angestellter> element extended with the gekuendigt flag:

Certainly, the XML developer will prefer an XML database like MarkLogic and have XML fragments delivered directly. Tektur, however, is for example implemented with MongoDB MongoDB, which works with JSON... Not least for this reason, I find JSON processing with XSLT quite exciting.
